concepts.name: d-Block Elements – Position in Periodic Table
concepts.explanation: The d-block occupies Groups 3 to 12 in the periodic table, where d orbitals of the penultimate energy level are progressively filled. There are four transition series: 3d (Sc to Zn), 4d (Y to Cd), 5d (La, Hf to Hg), and 6d (Ac, Rf to Cn).

concepts.name: Transition Elements – Definition and IUPAC Criteria
concepts.explanation: IUPAC defines transition metals as elements having incompletely filled d subshell either in the neutral atom or in any of their common oxidation states. Zn, Cd, and Hg (Group 12) are not considered transition metals as they have full d¹⁰ configuration in both ground state and common oxidation states.

concepts.name: Electronic Configurations of d-Block Elements
concepts.explanation: The general electronic configuration is (n-1)d¹⁻¹⁰ ns⁰⁻². Exceptions arise due to the small energy difference between (n-1)d and ns orbitals and the stability of half-filled (d⁵) and fully filled (d¹⁰) configurations. Examples: Cr: 3d⁵4s¹ (not 3d⁴4s²); Cu: 3d¹⁰4s¹ (not 3d⁹4s²); Pd: 4d¹⁰5s⁰.

concepts.name: Physical Properties of Transition Metals
concepts.explanation: Transition metals exhibit typical metallic properties: high tensile strength, ductility, malleability, thermal and electrical conductivity, and metallic lustre. They are hard, have high melting and boiling points (except Zn, Cd, Hg), and high enthalpies of atomisation.

concepts.name: Trends in Atomic and Ionic Sizes – Lanthanoid Contraction
concepts.explanation: Atomic and ionic radii decrease across a transition series due to increasing nuclear charge (poor shielding of d electrons). The radii of 5d series are almost identical to 4d series due to lanthanoid contraction (filling of 4f orbitals before 5d series reduces the expected size increase).

concepts.name: Lanthanoid Contraction – Definition and Consequences
concepts.explanation: Lanthanoid contraction is the steady decrease in size of lanthanoid ions (Ce³⁺ to Lu³⁺) due to poor shielding of 4f electrons. Consequences: (i) Zr and Hf have almost identical radii; (ii) separation of lanthanoids is difficult; (iii) basicity of hydroxides decreases from La³⁺ to Lu³⁺.

concepts.name: Ionisation Enthalpies of Transition Metals
concepts.explanation: Ionisation enthalpies increase gradually across a series due to increasing nuclear charge. The increase is less steep than for non-transition elements. Irregularities arise from the stability of d⁵ (half-filled) and d¹⁰ (fully filled) configurations. Example: Mn has lower third ionisation enthalpy due to d⁵ stability.

concepts.name: Oxidation States of Transition Metals
concepts.explanation: Transition metals exhibit a wide variety of oxidation states due to the involvement of both ns and (n-1)d electrons in bonding. The maximum oxidation state corresponds to the total number of s and d electrons up to Mn. Common oxidation states: Sc(+3), Ti(+2,+3,+4), V(+2 to +5), Cr(+2 to +6), Mn(+2 to +7), Fe(+2,+3), Co(+2,+3), Ni(+2), Cu(+1,+2), Zn(+2).

concepts.name: Standard Electrode Potentials of Transition Metals
concepts.explanation: The E°(M²⁺/M) values generally become less negative across the series. The irregular values for Mn, Ni, and Zn are due to the stability of d⁵ (Mn²⁺), hydration enthalpy (Ni²⁺), and d¹⁰ (Zn²⁺). Cu has a positive E° (+0.34 V), explaining its inability to liberate H₂ from acids.

concepts.name: Magnetic Properties of Transition Metal Ions
concepts.explanation: Transition metal ions are paramagnetic due to unpaired d electrons. Magnetic moment is calculated using the spin-only formula: μ = √[n(n+2)] BM, where n is the number of unpaired electrons. Diamagnetic ions have no unpaired electrons (d⁰, d¹⁰). Example: Mn²⁺ (d⁵) has μ = 5.92 BM.

concepts.name: Formation of Coloured Ions
concepts.explanation: Colour arises from d-d transitions: electrons in lower energy d orbitals absorb light and are excited to higher energy d orbitals. The colour observed is complementary to the colour absorbed. Examples: Ti³⁺ (purple), V³⁺ (green), Mn²⁺ (pink), Fe³⁺ (yellow), Co²⁺ (pink), Ni²⁺ (green), Cu²⁺ (blue).

concepts.name: Formation of Complex Compounds
concepts.explanation: Transition metals form a large number of complex compounds due to: (i) small size of metal ions; (ii) high ionic charges; (iii) availability of vacant d orbitals for bond formation. Examples: [Fe(CN)₆]³⁻, [Cu(NH₃)₄]²⁺, [PtCl₄]²⁻.

concepts.name: Catalytic Properties of Transition Metals
concepts.explanation: Transition metals and their compounds act as catalysts due to: (i) ability to adopt multiple oxidation states; (ii) ability to form complexes. Examples: V₂O₅ (Contact Process), Fe (Haber Process), Ni (hydrogenation), Fe³⁺ catalyses the reaction between iodide and persulphate ions.

concepts.name: Interstitial Compounds
concepts.explanation: Interstitial compounds are formed when small atoms (H, C, N) are trapped inside the crystal lattices of transition metals. They are non-stoichiometric. Characteristics: high melting points, very hard, retain metallic conductivity, chemically inert. Examples: TiC, MnN, Fe₃H, VH₀.₅₆.

concepts.name: Alloy Formation
concepts.explanation: Transition metals readily form alloys due to similar atomic radii (within ~15%). Alloys are hard and have high melting points. Examples: steel (Fe + C + Cr, Mn, W, V), stainless steel, brass (Cu + Zn), bronze (Cu + Sn).

concepts.name: Oxides and Oxoanions of Transition Metals
concepts.explanation: Transition metal oxides show variable acidic/basic character. Lower oxides are basic; higher oxides are acidic or amphoteric. Examples: CrO (basic), Cr₂O₃ (amphoteric), CrO₃ (acidic). Oxoanions: VO₄³⁻, CrO₄²⁻, MnO₄⁻. The highest oxidation state is stabilised by oxygen.

concepts.name: Potassium Dichromate (K₂Cr₂O₇) – Preparation
concepts.explanation: K₂Cr₂O₇ is prepared from chromite ore (FeCr₂O₄). Steps: (1) Fusion with Na₂CO₃ in air gives Na₂CrO₄; (2) Acidification converts chromate to dichromate: 2Na₂CrO₄ + 2H⁺ → Na₂Cr₂O₇ + H₂O; (3) Treatment with KCl gives K₂Cr₂O₇ (less soluble).

concepts.name: Potassium Dichromate – Structure and Properties
concepts.explanation: Chromate ion (CrO₄²⁻) is tetrahedral. Dichromate ion (Cr₂O₇²⁻) consists of two tetrahedra sharing one corner (Cr-O-Cr bond angle 126°). Chromate and dichromate are interconvertible: 2CrO₄²⁻ + 2H⁺ ⇌ Cr₂O₇²⁻ + H₂O.

concepts.name: Potassium Dichromate – Oxidising Action
concepts.explanation: In acidic medium, K₂Cr₂O₇ is a strong oxidising agent: Cr₂O₇²⁻ + 14H⁺ + 6e⁻ → 2Cr³⁺ + 7H₂O (E° = 1.33 V). It oxidises: (i) I⁻ to I₂; (ii) Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺; (iii) Sn²⁺ to Sn⁴⁺; (iv) H₂S to S; (v) SO₃²⁻ to SO₄²⁻.

concepts.name: Potassium Permanganate (KMnO₄) – Preparation
concepts.explanation: KMnO₄ is prepared from pyrolusite ore (MnO₂). Steps: (1) Fusion with KOH and KNO₃ gives green K₂MnO₄ (manganate); (2) In neutral/acidic solution, manganate disproportionates: 3MnO₄²⁻ + 4H⁺ → 2MnO₄⁻ + MnO₂ + 2H₂O. Commercially, electrolytic oxidation of manganate is used.

concepts.name: Potassium Permanganate – Structure and Properties
concepts.explanation: Permanganate ion (MnO₄⁻) is tetrahedral. Manganate ion (MnO₄²⁻) is also tetrahedral. KMnO₄ forms dark purple crystals. It decomposes on heating: 2KMnO₄ → K₂MnO₄ + MnO₂ + O₂. Manganate is paramagnetic (one unpaired electron), while permanganate is diamagnetic (no unpaired electron).

concepts.name: Potassium Permanganate – Oxidising Action in Acidic Medium
concepts.explanation: In acidic medium, MnO₄⁻ is reduced to Mn²⁺: MnO₄⁻ + 8H⁺ + 5e⁻ → Mn²⁺ + 4H₂O (E° = 1.52 V). It oxidises: (i) Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺; (ii) C₂O₄²⁻ to CO₂; (iii) I⁻ to I₂; (iv) SO₃²⁻ to SO₄²⁻; (v) NO₂⁻ to NO₃⁻; (vi) H₂S to S.

concepts.name: Potassium Permanganate – Oxidising Action in Neutral/Alkaline Medium
concepts.explanation: In neutral or faintly alkaline medium, MnO₄⁻ is reduced to MnO₂: MnO₄⁻ + 2H₂O + 3e⁻ → MnO₂ + 4OH⁻. It oxidises: (i) I⁻ to IO₃⁻; (ii) S₂O₃²⁻ to SO₄²⁻; (iii) Mn²⁺ to MnO₂.

concepts.name: f-Block Elements – Lanthanoids and Actinoids
concepts.explanation: The f-block consists of two series: lanthanoids (4f series, Ce to Lu, Z=58-71) and actinoids (5f series, Th to Lr, Z=90-103). They are placed separately at the bottom of the periodic table. Lanthanum (La) and Actinium (Ac) are often included in discussions.

concepts.name: Electronic Configurations of Lanthanoids
concepts.explanation: Lanthanoids have the general electronic configuration [Xe] 4f¹⁻¹⁴ 5d⁰⁻¹ 6s². The most stable oxidation state is +3, with electronic configuration [Xe] 4fⁿ. Exceptions arise due to stability of f⁰, f⁷, and f¹⁴ configurations. Examples: Ce (4f¹5d¹6s²), Gd (4f⁷5d¹6s²), Lu (4f¹⁴5d¹6s²).

concepts.name: Lanthanoid Contraction – Cause and Effects
concepts.explanation: Lanthanoid contraction is the steady decrease in size of lanthanoid ions (Ce³⁺ to Lu³⁺) due to poor shielding of 4f electrons. Effects: (i) Similar radii of 4d and 5d elements (Zr/Hf); (ii) Difficulty in separation of lanthanoids; (iii) Decrease in basicity of hydroxides from La(OH)₃ to Lu(OH)₃.

concepts.name: Oxidation States of Lanthanoids
concepts.explanation: The common oxidation state is +3. Some elements exhibit +2 (Eu, Yb) and +4 (Ce, Pr, Tb) states due to the stability of f⁷ and f¹⁴ configurations. Eu²⁺ (f⁷) and Yb²⁺ (f¹⁴) are stable; Ce⁴⁺ (f⁰) and Tb⁴⁺ (f⁷) are strong oxidants.

concepts.name: General Characteristics of Lanthanoids
concepts.explanation: Lanthanoids are silvery white soft metals that tarnish rapidly in air. They are good conductors of heat and electricity. They have high melting points (1000-1200 K). They form coloured ions (due to f-f transitions), are paramagnetic (except La³⁺, Lu³⁺), and are reactive (liberate H₂ from dilute acids).

concepts.name: Mischmetal and Uses of Lanthanoids
concepts.explanation: Mischmetal is an alloy of lanthanoid metals (~95%) and iron (~5%). Uses: (i) Mg-based alloys for bullets and shells; (ii) lighter flints; (iii) catalysts in petroleum cracking; (iv) phosphors in television screens.

concepts.name: Electronic Configurations of Actinoids
concepts.explanation: Actinoids have the general electronic configuration [Rn] 5f¹⁻¹⁴ 6d⁰⁻¹ 7s². The 5f orbitals are less buried than 4f orbitals and can participate more in bonding. Irregularities arise from the stability of f⁰, f⁷, and f¹⁴ configurations. Example: Am: [Rn] 5f⁷7s²; Cm: [Rn] 5f⁷6d¹7s².

concepts.name: Actinoid Contraction
concepts.explanation: Actinoid contraction is the steady decrease in size of actinoid ions across the series due to poor shielding of 5f electrons. It is greater than lanthanoid contraction because 5f electrons provide even poorer shielding than 4f electrons.

concepts.name: Oxidation States of Actinoids
concepts.explanation: Actinoids exhibit a greater range of oxidation states than lanthanoids due to comparable energies of 5f, 6d, and 7s orbitals. The common oxidation state is +3. Maximum oxidation state increases from +4 (Th) to +5 (Pa), +6 (U), +7 (Np), then decreases. Examples: U(VI), Np(VII).

concepts.name: General Characteristics of Actinoids
concepts.explanation: Actinoids are silvery, highly reactive metals (especially when finely divided). They react with boiling water to give oxide and hydride mixtures. They combine with most non-metals at moderate temperatures. They are radioactive; the later members have very short half-lives.

concepts.name: Comparison of Lanthanoids and Actinoids
concepts.explanation: Similarities: (i) Both show +3 oxidation state; (ii) Both show lanthanoid/actinoid contraction. Differences: (i) Actinoids have greater range of oxidation states; (ii) Actinoids are radioactive; (iii) Actinoids are more reactive; (iv) Magnetic properties of actinoids are more complex; (v) Ionisation enthalpies of early actinoids are lower than lanthanoids.

concepts.name: Applications of d- and f-Block Elements
concepts.explanation: d-block metals: (i) Iron and steel for construction; (ii) TiO₂ for pigments; (iii) MnO₂ for dry cells; (iv) AgBr for photography; (v) Catalysts (V₂O₅, Fe, Ni, PdCl₂, TiCl₄/Al(CH₃)₃ for Ziegler catalysis). f-block: (i) Mischmetal; (ii) Nuclear energy (Th, U); (iii) Phosphors.
